name: Save Highfields Library Hours

input.problem: I want Leicester City Council to reverse the planned closure of the branch library in Highfields and keep it open with its current opening hours.

sources.claim: LE2 1TH → ward Castle, constituency Leicester South, local authority Leicester

Leicester City Council's original April 2025 proposals identified Highfields as one of 12 sites to be retained and rebranded as a library 'hub', with potential for 40 staffed hours a week — not as a site proposed for closure.

research.refinedProblem: Contrary to the campaign's original premise, verified council records show Highfields Library was not on Leicester City Council's closure list. Following a 2025 consultation on library and community centre cuts, the Council's March 2026 decision confirms Highfields Library continues to be run by the Council as a 'library hub' with staffed opening hours, while a different site (Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library) is the one being closed. The real, verifiable issue for campaigners is therefore whether Highfields Library's revised staffed opening hours and service model (self-access hours, reduced staff numbers) match its 'current' pre-2025 hours, not an outright closure.

location.geography: Highfields Library, 98 Melbourne Road, Leicester, LE2 — inner-city Leicester neighbourhood

research.interpretation: The user wants Leicester City Council to reverse a 'planned closure' of Highfields branch library and restore/keep current opening hours. Research shows the premise needs correcting: Council documents describe Highfields as one of the sites retained as a library 'hub' under the 2025-26 restructuring, not one proposed for closure or community transfer. The user's underlying concern is best understood as opposing any reduction in staffed opening hours or service level at Highfields as part of the Council's approved savings programme.

Does the 11 March/26 March 2026 decision notice specify Highfields' final staffed hours, or only the citywide 30–40 hrs/week banding?

context.situation: Leicester City Council ran a citywide consultation (April–June 2025) on proposals to restructure library and community centre provision to meet budget savings targets, initially covering withdrawal of funding from 11 sites and reduced staffed hours at a further 13. Highfields was one of 12 sites proposed to continue as a council-run 'library hub' with 40 staffed hours a week, not one proposed for closure or community transfer.
context.currentPolicy: Following consultation, revised proposals were approved: the Council continues to directly run 15 libraries (including Highfields) and 3 neighbourhood centres, delivering reduced savings of £1.57m/year (down from an initial £2.1m/year target) via smaller staff reductions, self-service access hours at some sites, and Community Asset Transfer for other community centres. Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library — not Highfields — is the site confirmed to close, due to low usage and poor building condition.
affected: Library and community centre staff facing reduced staff numbers/redeployment
Community groups considering Community Asset Transfer of other (non-Highfields) sites
Fosse Neighbourhood Centre/Library users and Alice Hawkins Community Projects (the group being relocated from that closing site)
keyDates: 2 April – 29 June 2025: full public consultation on library/community centre proposals
November 2025: Culture and Neighbourhoods Scrutiny Committee considered assessment/recommendations report
11 March 2026: original portfolio holder decision taken
26 March 2026: Full Council considered a call-in of the decision and upheld it, reviving the original decision in full
Implementation described as beginning 'in the next few months' after March 2026
institutions: Leicester City Council Cabinet / Assistant City Mayor for Libraries and Community Centres
Alice Hawkins Community Projects (affected by Fosse closure, not Highfields)
context.howItChanged: The original campaign brief assumed Highfields Library faced closure. Research found no evidence of this: council consultation and decision documents consistently list Highfields as a retained 'hub' site with staffed hours, while a different library (Fosse) is the one being closed. This substantially reframes the campaign from 'reverse a closure' to 'scrutinise/challenge any reduction in Highfields' staffed opening hours or service level' as the approved model is implemented.

decisionMaker.practical: Decisions are taken via portfolio-holder decision notices, informed by consultation findings reports and Equality Impact Assessments, and can be reviewed via the Council's call-in/scrutiny process before being revived if upheld

Culture and Neighbourhoods Scrutiny Committee review, November 2025
Call-in under Rule 12 of the Council's Constitution, considered and upheld by full Council, 26 March 2026
Community Asset Transfer process for sites being offered to community groups (not applicable to Highfields, which remains council-run)
interventionPoints: Engagement with the Assistant City Mayor for Libraries and Community Centres and local ward councillors as implementation proceeds
Monitoring of building-specific implementation plans (a 'Highfields library building pack' document exists on the council's decision-support pages) for confirmed final hours
Formal Council petition or public questions at Full Council/Scrutiny meetings
Continued alliance with UNISON Leicester City branch and citywide campaign groups opposing cuts
deadlines: No further public consultation window identified; the decision was upheld on 26 March 2026 and implementation is described as starting within a few months of that date
unresolved: Whether Highfields' final approved staffed hours match, exceed, or fall short of its pre-2025 hours
Whether any site-specific (Highfields-only) decision point remains open for campaigners to influence

refinedProblem: Contrary to the campaign's original premise, verified council records show Highfields Library was not on Leicester City Council's closure list. Following a 2025 consultation on library and community centre cuts, the Council's March 2026 decision confirms Highfields Library continues to be run by the Council as a 'library hub' with staffed opening hours, while a different site (Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library) is the one being closed. The real, verifiable issue for campaigners is therefore whether Highfields Library's revised staffed opening hours and service model (self-access hours, reduced staff numbers) match its 'current' pre-2025 hours, not an outright closure.
interpretation: The user wants Leicester City Council to reverse a 'planned closure' of Highfields branch library and restore/keep current opening hours. Research shows the premise needs correcting: Council documents describe Highfields as one of the sites retained as a library 'hub' under the 2025-26 restructuring, not one proposed for closure or community transfer. The user's underlying concern is best understood as opposing any reduction in staffed opening hours or service level at Highfields as part of the Council's approved savings programme.

objective.dm: Assistant City Mayor for Libraries and Community Centres, Cllr Vi Dempster, acting for Leicester City Council as statutory library authority
objective.action: publicly confirm Highfields Library's final staffed opening hours and commit that they will be maintained at no less than the pre-2025 staffed level, with a formal usage-based review point after 12 months before any further reduction
objective.by: before the approved 'library hub' model is implemented at Highfields (implementation described as starting within a few months of the 26 March 2026 decision — target end of autumn 2026)
objective.mvw: the Council publishes Highfields' confirmed staffed vs self-access hours side-by-side with the pre-2025 baseline and agrees a monitored 12-month review, even if the initial hours figure is not yet improved
objective.success: a written commitment from the Assistant City Mayor or Neighbourhood Services confirming Highfields staffed hours and a review mechanism, evidenced in a decision-support document or letter to the campaign
constraints: The core decision was taken 11 March 2026 and upheld on call-in 26 March 2026 — the campaign is operating at implementation stage, not consultation stage
Highfields is a retained/funded site, not a closure — the 'reverse the closure' framing is false and must be dropped
Exact pre-2025 and post-implementation staffed hours are not yet confirmed and must be established before any numerical demand is fixed
£1.57m/year savings target is approved council policy under wider budget pressure

smart.assessment: Targets a single named site (Highfields Library, 98 Melbourne Road), a single service dimension (staffed opening hours vs self-access), and a named decision-holder
Success is a published hours comparison plus a written review commitment — both binary, checkable outputs
Realistic because implementation detail is still being finalised and a building pack exists; a transparency-and-review ask is far cheaper for the Council than reopening the whole savings decision
Directly addresses the verified real issue (staffed-hours reduction at a retained hub), not the disproven closure premise
Tied to the implementation window beginning within months of March 2026, giving a concrete autumn 2026 deadline

plan.statusQuoCost: For Cllr Vi Dempster and the Council, quietly implementing reduced staffed hours at Highfields is currently low-cost because the decision is legally settled and the closure narrative is (correctly) disproven. The campaign's job is to raise that cost precisely at implementation: by pinning her own 'protecting services' words to a measurable Highfields commitment, exposing any staffed-hours reduction against a published baseline, testing the equality assessment for a deprived inner-city ward, and generating visible local resident and union backing — so that publishing the hours and agreeing a review becomes cheaper and safer than being seen to cut a service she promised to protect.

strategy.narrative: Highfields Library is not closing — and that is exactly why this fight is winnable. The Council chose to keep it as a staffed 'library hub'. Now Highfields residents simply want the Council to keep its word: tell us the hours in black and white, and guarantee our staffed library does not lose the hours our children, elders and job-seekers rely on. We are holding Cllr Dempster to her own promise that this was about protecting services, not cutting them.

lobbying.briefing: Highfields Library (98 Melbourne Road, LE2) is NOT closing. Leicester City Council's approved March 2026 decision retains Highfields as one of the libraries it continues to run directly, as part of a 'library hub' model. The site that is closing is Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library, due to low usage and building condition — not Highfields. This means the correct campaign issue is staffed opening hours at Highfields under the new hub model, not reversing a closure. The Council's original April 2025 proposals identified Highfields as a hub site with potential for up to 40 staffed hours a week, but the exact pre-2025 baseline and the final confirmed staffed hours under the March 2026 decision have not yet been published or verified by this campaign [VERIFY: exact pre-2025 staffed hours at Highfields] [VERIFY: exact confirmed staffed hours at Highfields post-implementation]. A 'Highfields library building pack' dated February 2026 exists among the Council's own decision-support documents and references 'Current Staffed Hours' for the site, so the data exists — it simply needs to be made public and compared side by side. Cllr Vi Dempster, Assistant City Mayor for Libraries and Community Centres, told leicester.news on 26 November 2025 that 'this was never a closure programme, it was about finding ways to protect these services.' The campaign's strategy is to hold her to that statement: ask her to publish the Highfields hours comparison and commit to a 12-month usage-based review before any further reduction, rather than to reopen a legally settled decision. UNISON Leicester City branch already opposes the cuts programme on statutory-duty grounds (Public Libraries and Museums Act 1964). The City Mayor's name and the Highfields ward councillors' names could not be verified in research and must not be used in public-facing material until confirmed [VERIFY: current City Mayor of Leicester] [VERIFY: Highfields ward councillor names].
lobbying.meetingEmail:
Subject: Request for a meeting — Highfields Library staffed hours

Dear Cllr Dempster,

We write on behalf of Save Highfields Library, a group of residents and library users from the Highfields area (LE2).

We understand Highfields Library, 98 Melbourne Road, will continue to be run by the Council as a library hub under the decision approved on 11 March 2026 and upheld on call-in on 26 March 2026. We are not asking you to reverse that decision. Our concern is narrower and, we believe, straightforward to address: residents want to know the confirmed staffed opening hours at Highfields once the hub model is implemented, and how these compare with the hours in place before 2025.

You told leicester.news on 26 November 2025 that this programme 'was never a closure programme, it was about finding ways to protect these services.' We would welcome the opportunity to discuss how that commitment can be made visible and measurable at Highfields specifically, ahead of the hub model going live.

We would be grateful for a meeting with you or a relevant Neighbourhood Services officer in the coming weeks to discuss:
1) publishing Highfields' confirmed staffed vs self-access hours alongside the pre-2025 baseline, and
2) agreeing a monitored 12-month review point before any further reduction in staffed hours.

We are happy to meet at a time and location convenient to you, including at the library itself.

Yours sincerely,
Save Highfields Library (campaign spokesperson)
lobbying.agenda:
Meeting: Highfields Library staffed hours — Save Highfields Library and Cllr Vi Dempster (or delegated officer)

1. Introductions and shared starting point: Highfields is a retained, funded library hub, not a closure (2 mins)
2. Council's current position: what staffed vs self-access hours are confirmed for Highfields, and against what pre-2025 baseline (10 mins)
3. Campaign ask: publish the hours comparison publicly (10 mins)
4. Campaign ask: agree a 12-month usage-based review point before any further reduction (10 mins)
5. Discussion of the Highfields-specific Equality Impact Assessment and how usage evidence will be considered (10 mins)
6. Role for ward councillors and the Culture and Neighbourhoods Scrutiny Committee in monitoring (5 mins)
7. Agreed next steps and timeline for a written response (5 mins)
keyArguments: Highfields is a retained, Council-run library hub under the decision taken on 11 March 2026 and upheld on 26 March 2026 — the site facing closure is Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library, not Highfields, so this is a staffed-hours issue, not a closure fight.

Cllr Dempster's own public statement — that the programme 'was never a closure programme, it was about finding ways to protect these services' — commits the Council to a standard that a simple, published hours comparison and review point would let it demonstrably meet.

The Council's original April 2025 proposals identified Highfields as a hub site with potential for up to 40 staffed hours a week, indicating the Council itself has already modelled a staffed-hours figure for this site that residents are entitled to see confirmed.

A 'Highfields library building pack' dated February 2026, produced by the Council as part of its own decision-making materials, already references current staffed hours for the site — the data needed to answer this ask already exists internally.

The Council has a statutory duty under the Public Libraries and Museums Act 1964 to provide a comprehensive and efficient library service, cited by UNISON Leicester City branch in opposing the wider cuts programme — a duty that is easier to demonstrate compliance with if hours are transparently published and reviewed.

The Council itself describes its consultation as one of its most extensive, with 4,989 surveys and 41 stakeholder groups engaged — a transparency-and-review ask at implementation is a low-cost way of honouring that legitimacy claim for a specific, high-need inner-city site.
talkingPoints: We are not asking the Council to reverse the March 2026 decision or reopen Highfields as it was — we accept it is being retained as a hub.
Our single, specific ask is: publish Highfields' confirmed staffed hours next to the pre-2025 baseline, and commit to review usage after 12 months before any further reduction.
This is squarely in line with what the Council has already said publicly about protecting services, not cutting them.
The building pack the Council already produced in February 2026 shows this data exists — we are asking for publication, not new work.
Highfields serves a deprived inner-city ward; residents, especially children, older people and job-seekers, rely on staffed access, not self-service alone.
A review point after 12 months protects both the Council's budget position and residents' interests — it is a shared-risk mechanism, not a reopening of the savings decision.
questionsToAsk: What are Highfields Library's confirmed staffed opening hours under the approved hub model, and what were staffed hours immediately before 2025?
Will the Council commit to publishing this comparison, and by what date?
Was a Highfields-specific Equality Impact Assessment carried out on the staffed-hours change, and can residents see it?
Who in Neighbourhood Services holds the final building pack data for Highfields, and can it be shared directly with the campaign?
Will the Council agree to a formal 12-month review point, tied to usage data, before any further reduction in Highfields' staffed hours?
Can the Culture and Neighbourhoods Scrutiny Committee be asked to receive a monitoring update on Highfields hours as part of implementation oversight?
Who are the current ward councillors for Highfields, so we can involve them directly in this discussion?

objections.objection: This decision was already taken and upheld on call-in on 26 March 2026 — it isn't up for revisiting.
objections.response: We agree, and we are not asking you to revisit the decision itself. Our ask is narrower: publish the confirmed Highfields hours against the pre-2025 baseline, and agree a review point. That's implementation transparency, not a reversal.

objections.objection: Highfields was never going to close — this campaign is based on a false premise.
objections.response: We accept that and have corrected our own framing: Highfields is retained as a hub, and the site actually closing is Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library. Our concern now is specifically about staffed hours at Highfields, which is a separate and legitimate question.

objections.objection: The Council ran one of its most extensive consultations and already accounted for local views.
objections.response: We recognise that scale of engagement — 4,989 surveys and 41 stakeholder groups is significant. That is exactly why we think publishing the Highfields-specific hours outcome, and monitoring it against real usage for 12 months, is a natural extension of that consultative approach, not a challenge to it.

objections.objection: We can't commit to a review that might reopen budget decisions later.
objections.response: The review would look at usage evidence, not automatically reopen the savings figure. It gives the Council a chance to show hours are working for residents, or to make an informed, evidenced decision if they are not — which supports good governance either way.

objections.objection: We don't have the exact figures to hand right now.
objections.response: That's understood — the February 2026 building pack indicates this data exists internally. We're not asking for an answer in this meeting, only a commitment to publish it and a realistic timeframe for doing so.

media.pressRelease:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Highfields residents call on council to publish library hours pledge and guarantee no cut below current staffed service

Leicester, LE2 — A resident group, Save Highfields Library, is calling on Leicester City Council to publicly confirm the future staffed opening hours at Highfields Library, 98 Melbourne Road, and to commit that staffed hours will not fall below the level residents currently rely on — with a 12-month usage review before any further reduction is considered.

Highfields is not among the sites earmarked for closure. The council's own decision confirms it will continue to directly run 15 libraries, including Highfields, as part of a 'library hub' model (Leicester City Council, 6 March 2026). The site actually closing under the approved plan is Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library, not Highfields (Leicester City Council, 6 March 2026).

But residents say the council has not yet published the exact staffed hours that will apply at Highfields once the hub model is implemented, despite a site-specific 'building pack' produced by the council in February 2026 that refers to current staffed hours. [VERIFY: exact pre-2025 and post-implementation staffed hours at Highfields].

The council's Assistant City Mayor for Libraries and Community Centres, Cllr Vi Dempster, told leicester.news in November 2025 that the changes were "never a closure programme, it was about finding ways to protect these services in the face of ongoing budget cuts." Save Highfields Library says it is holding the council to that promise.

"We're not asking the council to reverse a decision — we know Highfields is staying open as a hub," said a campaign spokesperson. "We're asking them to show us, in black and white, what 'protecting services' actually means for our library's staffed hours, and to promise not to quietly cut them further without checking how the community is using the service first."

UNISON Leicester City branch, which represents library staff, has previously opposed cuts to the service, citing the council's statutory duty under the Public Libraries and Museums Act 1964 to provide "a comprehensive and efficient library service for all persons" (UNISON Leicester City branch, 24 April 2025).

The council undertook what it describes as one of its most extensive consultations, with 4,989 surveys and 1,053 people attending public meetings (Leicester City Council). Save Highfields Library argues that legitimacy should now extend to transparent implementation at site level.

The group is asking Cllr Dempster to:
1. Publish Highfields' confirmed staffed hours alongside the pre-2025 baseline; and
2. Commit to a monitored 12-month review before any further reduction.

ENDS

Notes to editors:
- Save Highfields Library is a resident-led group based around Highfields Library, 98 Melbourne Road, Leicester LE2.
- [VERIFY: contact details for Save Highfields Library spokesperson to be added by the group before distribution.]
- The campaign does not dispute that Highfields will remain open as a staffed library hub; its concern is solely the level of staffed hours and transparency around them.

media.headline: Highfields residents demand council publish library hours pledge, not just a promise to 'protect services'
altAngles: Data angle: 'The council knows the numbers — residents don't.' Focus on the unpublished February 2026 building pack and call for an information request/FOI response.
Accountability angle: 'Cllr Dempster said it was never about cuts — so prove it.' Focus on holding a named decision-maker to her own quoted words.

Fairness angle: 'A £2.5m investment, but is Highfields' staffed time being quietly squeezed?' Contrast the council's headline investment figure with the lack of a published site-level hours commitment.

Statutory duty angle: with UNISON, focus on the 1964 Act duty to provide a comprehensive and efficient library service and whether reduced staffed hours in a deprived inner-city ward is consistent with that duty.
Process angle: given the scale of the council's consultation (nearly 5,000 surveys), why hasn't the same transparency been applied to telling residents the actual hours at their own local library?
media.spokespeople: All quotes below are drafts to be adapted and attributed by real people who have given informed consent — for example a 'campaign spokesperson' from Save Highfields Library, or 'a parent' / 'a library user' with direct experience. No named councillor, council officer or journalist should be quoted or invented. Do not attribute quotes to Cllr Vi Dempster, the City Mayor or any ward councillor unless they have actually provided that comment; where their name is used it must be confirmed via an official source first.

quotes.quote: We're not fighting a closure that isn't happening. We're asking the council to do the one thing it hasn't done yet: tell us, in writing, what staffed hours Highfields will actually have — and promise not to quietly cut them further without checking with the people who use the library.
quotes.note: Anchors the campaign's corrected framing; safe to use as-is or adapt.

quotes.quote: My kids come here after school because it's staffed, warm and safe. If those hours shrink without anyone telling us, we lose that quietly, one week at a time.
quotes.note: Illustrative testimony draft — must be replaced with a real parent's own words and consent obtained.

quotes.quote: I don't use the self-service machines easily. Staffed hours are the difference between me using this library and not using it at all.
quotes.note: Draft only; needs a genuine resident's voice and explicit consent before use.

quotes.quote: The council has a statutory duty to provide a comprehensive and efficient library service. That duty doesn't disappear once a decision is taken — it has to be checked at the point services are actually delivered.
quotes.note: Reflects UNISON's verified 24 April 2025 public statement citing the 1964 Act; to be confirmed and re-issued by an actual UNISON rep.

qa.a: No. The council's approved March 2026 decision confirms it will continue to directly run 15 libraries, including Highfields, as a staffed 'library hub' (Leicester City Council, 6 March 2026). The site closing under this decision is Fosse Neighbourhood Centre and Library, not Highfields.

qa.q: What exactly is the campaign asking for?
qa.a: Two things: (1) publication of Highfields' confirmed staffed opening hours alongside the pre-2025 baseline hours, and (2) a written commitment to a monitored 12-month review before any further reduction in staffed hours.

qa.q: What are the current or future staffed hours at Highfields?
qa.a: [VERIFY: exact figures]. A council 'building pack' document from February 2026 refers to current staffed hours at the site, but the specific figures have not yet been made public in a form the campaign can cite. This is precisely the information the campaign is asking the council to release.

qa.q: Why now, if the decision was already taken?
qa.a: The core decision was taken on 11 March 2026 and upheld on call-in on 26 March 2026. The campaign is not seeking to reopen that decision — it is focused on the implementation stage, where the exact staffed-hours model for Highfields is still being finalised, expected within a few months of the decision.
A resident-led group of library users, parents and local residents around Highfields Library on Melbourne Road, working alongside UNISON Leicester City branch on the statutory-duty case.

qa.q: Does this campaign support the wider anti-cuts campaigns, e.g. from opposition parties?
qa.a: The campaign welcomes anyone amplifying the specific Highfields hours ask but is resident-led and cross-party. It is not aligned with any political party's wider campaign.

hostileQA.q: Isn't this campaign based on a false premise — Highfields was never going to close?
hostileQA.a: That's correct, and we've corrected our framing to reflect it. Our concern now is specifically about staffed hours at a site the council itself has chosen to keep open, and about the council publishing that information transparently.

hostileQA.q: The council ran one of its most extensive consultations — 4,989 surveys, over 1,000 meeting attendees. Isn't this decided?
hostileQA.a: We're not asking the council to redo the citywide decision. We're asking it to apply the same transparency at implementation stage for one specific site — publishing the actual hours figure it already holds.

hostileQA.q: How do you know staffed hours are even being cut? You admit you don't have the figures.
hostileQA.a: We don't know yet, and we say so clearly. That's exactly the problem: the council holds a February 2026 document referring to current staffed hours at Highfields but hasn't published a clear before-and-after comparison. Publishing it would resolve this immediately, one way or the other.

hostileQA.q: Are you working with the Conservative 'Protect Our Libraries' campaign?
hostileQA.a: No. We are a resident-led, cross-party group. Other campaigns are free to amplify our specific ask, but we are not aligned with or coordinated by any political party.

hostileQA.q: Isn't a 12-month review just a way of avoiding the real fight over cuts?
hostileQA.a: We see it as a realistic, checkable win: if hours are maintained, a review confirms that and reassures residents; if they are quietly reduced, a review gives us and the council evidence to act on before it becomes permanent.

hostileQA.q: Have you named the councillors responsible for this?
hostileQA.a: We are being deliberately careful here. Some individuals' identities — such as ward councillors — have not yet been verified through an official source, and we will not name anyone publicly until that is confirmed.

media.timing: Hold this release until the FOI/building-pack information request response is in hand or a clear refusal is confirmed (target: within 20 working days of submission), so the story has a verified factual hook rather than resting on unverified figures. Align publication with either (a) a response/non-response from Cllr Dempster's office following the private letter, or (b) the next Culture and Neighbourhoods Scrutiny Committee cycle, whichever comes first — this maximises the 'council won't say' or 'council has agreed to review' news hook. Avoid launch during school holidays when parent testimony and local media bandwidth are lower.
